useCases.name: Finance Approvals
useCases.tagline: Close the books faster. Stop chasing approvals across five systems.
useCases.pattern: Finance teams at scaling companies spend more time chasing approvals than analyzing the numbers. A single PO approval touches the ERP, email, Slack, and a procurement portal -- and someone has to manually stitch it together.
useCases.gap: When finance tries to automate approvals, they get one-system bots that break the moment the process crosses into the ERP or a legacy tool. The exception handling always lands back on a human.
useCases.proof: A mid-market manufacturer automated AP approvals, vendor onboarding, and expense exception handling with Orchestrate -- reducing manual processing time by 65% and cutting average approval cycle from 4 days to 6 hours.
useCases.validation: One real approval workflow automated end-to-end in 14 days. Success metric: cycle time and FTE hours before vs. after.
compellingEvents: Quarter close crunch where approvals are the bottleneck
New CFO mandate to reduce cost per transaction
ERP migration creating a window to re-architect workflows
Audit finding around manual controls in AP process
Headcount freeze with transaction volume growing
useCases.killerQuestion: When your team closes the books at quarter end, what's the one approval process that always causes the last-minute scramble?

Situation: Walk me through a typical PO approval from request to sign-off. How many systems does it touch and how many humans have to act?
What percentage of your team's time goes into approval workflows versus actual financial analysis?
How many approval exceptions does your team manually handle per week -- the ones that don't fit the standard workflow?
Pain: When an approval gets stuck -- because the approver is out, or it crossed a threshold, or it hit an exception -- what actually happens? Who picks it up?
What does a delayed AP approval cost you when a vendor puts you on hold or you miss an early-payment discount?
Which approval processes are your team doing manually today that you'd automate tomorrow if you could?
Impact: If you could cut your average approval cycle time by 70%, what would your team do with that recovered capacity?
What's the dollar value of early-payment discounts you're leaving on the table because approvals don't move fast enough?
If you had to put a number on the cost of one delayed quarter-close -- in overtime, audit risk, and leadership time -- what would it be?
Critical Event: Is there a quarter-end, audit cycle, or board reporting deadline where approval delays are going to create real pain in the next 90 days?
Has your CFO tied any headcount or budget decisions to demonstrating improved finance process efficiency this year?
Is there an ERP upgrade or migration on the roadmap that would create a natural window to re-architect these workflows?
Decision: If we showed you a working proof on your real AP process in 14 days, who in the org would need to sign off on moving it to production?
Does this decision live with finance leadership, or does IT need to be in the room because of the ERP integration?

objections.objection: Our ERP already has workflow automation built in.
objections.response: That's exactly the issue -- the ERP handles what stays inside the ERP. The moment an approval touches email, a procurement portal, or a downstream system, the built-in workflow hands it to a human. Orchestrate completes the cross-system leg without a handoff.

objections.objection: We tried RPA for this and it broke every time something changed.
objections.response: RPA is brittle because it screen-scrapes. Orchestrate uses native APIs and adapts when system interfaces change. The reason your RPA broke is the same reason we exist -- rule-based bots can't handle exceptions. Orchestrate reasons through them.

objections.objection: Finance won't trust an AI making approval decisions.
objections.response: It doesn't make decisions -- it executes them. The human still sets the policy and approves exceptions. Orchestrate handles the routing, chasing, escalation, and data entry. The CFO still decides. They just stop waiting 4 days to get the paperwork in front of them.

useCases.name: HR Service Delivery
useCases.tagline: Give HR back to HR. Stop letting your team be a help desk.
useCases.pattern: HR teams at 2,000+ person companies spend the majority of their time on transactional requests -- benefits questions, onboarding paperwork, leave requests -- work that should be automated but spans too many systems for a single bot to handle.
useCases.gap: HRIS, payroll, benefits portal, and email all hold different parts of the employee record. Every cross-system request requires a human to bridge them. No single HR automation tool owns all four.
useCases.proof: A global financial services firm deployed Orchestrate across HR shared services, automating onboarding, benefits enrollment, and policy Q&A -- reducing tier-1 HR ticket volume by 58% and freeing 14 FTEs to focus on talent strategy.
useCases.validation: One real HR workflow -- onboarding or leave management -- automated in 14 days with ticket volume and resolution time measured.
compellingEvents: New CHRO with a mandate to modernize HR operations
Headcount freeze requiring HR to do more with same team
High HR staff turnover creating institutional knowledge loss
HRIS migration creating a window to redesign workflows
Employee satisfaction scores dropping on HR responsiveness
useCases.killerQuestion: What percentage of your HR team's week goes to answering questions that are already in the policy document?

Situation: What does a new hire's first two weeks look like from an HR operations standpoint? How many systems does your team touch to complete onboarding?
What are your top 3 HR ticket categories by volume? Are those the same as your top 3 by time spent?
How do employees currently get answers to benefits or policy questions -- and what happens when the answer requires action across systems?
Pain: When a leave request comes in that touches HRIS, payroll, and the manager's calendar, who coordinates that, and how long does it take?
What HR work is your team doing manually today that they're embarrassed to admit hasn't been automated yet?
How many of your HR staff's hours each week go to work that a well-configured AI could handle?
Impact: If your HR team recovered 40% of their time from transactional work, what strategic work would they actually do?
What's the cost of a poor onboarding experience when it affects new hire retention in the first 90 days?
When your CHRO talks about HR transformation, what's the metric they're actually being measured on?
Critical Event: Is there a headcount freeze that's requiring HR to absorb more work without adding people?
Is there a HRIS migration or open enrollment period coming up that would create urgency to get automation in place first?
Has employee sentiment around HR responsiveness shown up in a recent engagement survey?
Decision: Would a decision like this sit with HR leadership, or does it require IT sign-off because of the HRIS integration?
If we ran a 14-day proof on your onboarding workflow and the results were strong, what does the path to full deployment look like?

objections.objection: We already have ServiceNow handling HR tickets.
objections.response: ServiceNow manages the ticket -- Orchestrate completes the work behind it. When a leave request comes in through ServiceNow, the agent still has to go into HRIS, update payroll, notify the manager. Orchestrate does that execution automatically, so ServiceNow closes the ticket faster.

objections.objection: Employees won't trust an AI for sensitive HR questions.
objections.response: The experience is a conversation, not a form. And for anything sensitive -- performance, terminations, medical leave -- the agent hands off to a human immediately. The automation handles the transactional 70%, the humans own the relational 30%.

useCases.name: IT Service Desk
useCases.tagline: Resolve L1 tickets without a human in the loop.
useCases.pattern: IT service desks at mid-to-large enterprises resolve the same 200 issues over and over. Password resets, access requests, software provisioning -- 60% of ticket volume is predictable and repetitive, but resolution still requires a human to touch four systems.
useCases.gap: ITSM tools manage the ticket but don't resolve it. The agent still has to go into Active Directory, the provisioning system, the asset database, and email to complete the work. Orchestrate completes the resolution automatically.
useCases.proof: A global manufacturing company automated L1 and L2 ticket resolution with Orchestrate across their IT service desk, reducing average resolution time from 2.3 days to 4 hours and deflecting 52% of tickets from human agents entirely.
useCases.validation: One ticket category automated end-to-end (e.g. access requests) in 14 days with resolution time and deflection rate measured.
compellingEvents: IT headcount freeze with ticket volume growing post-merger
New CIO mandate to reduce cost per ticket
Merger creating IT integration work that's swamping the service desk
SLA breach history triggering leadership scrutiny
ITSM platform migration creating a redesign window
useCases.killerQuestion: What are your top 5 ticket categories by volume, and of those, how many could be resolved without a human if the right systems were connected?

Situation: What does ticket resolution look like for your top categories? Walk me through what an agent actually does to resolve a password reset or an access request.
What percentage of your ticket volume is L1 and L2 today? And of that, how much is the same request type repeating?
How many systems does an agent touch to resolve your most common ticket types?
Pain: When your SLA is breached, what's usually the root cause -- volume, staffing, or the manual steps between systems?
What happens to your service desk at 2am when a ticket comes in and no one's staffed? How is that handled today?
Which ticket types does your team complain about most because they take the most time relative to the complexity of the fix?
Impact: What does a 1-point improvement in your CSAT score translate to in terms of business impact or IT credibility with the business?
If you cut your average resolution time by 60%, what's the operational value -- in SLA compliance, agent morale, and cost per ticket?
How much of your IT budget goes to service desk staffing versus higher-value engineering work?
Critical Event: Is there a merger, acquisition, or headcount integration putting pressure on your service desk in the next 6 months?
Is there an ITSM platform renewal or migration on the roadmap where you could redesign the resolution layer at the same time?
Has a recent SLA breach or audit created pressure from leadership to demonstrate operational improvement?
Decision: For something like this, does the decision sit with IT operations leadership or does the CISO need to be involved because of the Active Directory and provisioning access?
If we ran a proof on your top ticket category in 14 days and hit your resolution time target, who signs off on production deployment?

objections.objection: We already have a virtual agent / chatbot on the service desk.
objections.response: The chatbot deflects the question. Orchestrate resolves the ticket. When an employee asks for access to a system, a chatbot confirms the request and routes it. Orchestrate goes into Active Directory, provisions the access, confirms back to the employee, and closes the ticket -- no human needed.

objections.objection: Security will never allow an AI agent to have access to Active Directory.
objections.response: The agent operates with the same least-privilege access model as a service account. It can only do what the policy explicitly allows. Your security team defines the guardrails -- Orchestrate operates within them. We bring IBM's enterprise security architecture to the conversation.

useCases.name: Supply Chain Operations
useCases.tagline: Automate the coordination work that falls between your supply chain systems.
useCases.pattern: Supply chain teams spend enormous time on coordination work -- supplier communications, PO exceptions, inventory updates, logistics tracking -- that spans ERP, supplier portals, logistics platforms, and email. No system owns the full chain.
useCases.gap: Each system handles its piece, but the handoffs between them require humans. When a PO has an exception, someone manually emails the supplier, updates the ERP, notifies the warehouse, and adjusts the forecast. Orchestrate does all four steps as one automated flow.
useCases.proof: A consumer goods manufacturer automated PO exception handling and supplier communication workflows with Orchestrate, reducing manual coordination time by 70% and cutting average exception resolution from 3 days to same-day.
useCases.validation: One supply chain workflow -- PO exceptions or supplier onboarding -- automated end-to-end in 14 days.
compellingEvents: Supply chain disruption exposing coordination gaps
New COO mandate to reduce operational cost per unit
ERP migration creating a window to redesign workflows
Supplier base expansion increasing coordination volume
Inventory accuracy issues traced to manual data entry errors
useCases.killerQuestion: When a PO exception hits, how many people touch it before it's resolved, and how long does that take?

Situation: Walk me through what happens when a supplier misses a delivery date. Who gets notified, in what order, and what systems get updated?
How much of your supply chain team's week is spent on coordination and communication tasks versus strategic work?
How many supplier interactions per week require manual intervention from your operations team?
Pain: When an exception hits a PO -- wrong quantity, late delivery, quality hold -- what's the actual process for resolving it? How many handoffs?
Which supply chain coordination tasks are your team doing manually that they know should be automated but nobody's had time to fix?
What breaks down first when volume spikes? Is it the coordination layer that can't scale?
Impact: What's the cost of a 3-day delay in resolving a PO exception -- in expediting fees, production downtime, or customer impact?
If you could cut manual coordination time by 60%, how would you redeploy that capacity in your operations team?
What does inventory accuracy at 98%+ versus 94% mean for your working capital and service levels?
Critical Event: Is there a peak season, product launch, or supplier consolidation event in the next 90 days that's going to stress your coordination capacity?
Is there an ERP upgrade or supply chain platform migration on the roadmap that would create a natural window to redesign these workflows?
Has a recent disruption or audit exposed coordination gaps that leadership is now watching?
Decision: Would a decision like this sit with supply chain leadership, or does procurement and IT need to be aligned before anything moves?
If we automated your PO exception workflow in a 14-day proof and showed the time savings, who would champion moving it to production?

objections.objection: Our ERP handles supplier communications.
objections.response: The ERP sends the notification. Orchestrate handles the conversation. When a supplier responds with a revised date, requests a partial shipment, or escalates an issue, someone has to process that response and update four systems. Orchestrate does that response handling automatically.

objections.objection: Our suppliers won't interact with an AI.
objections.response: The supplier experience is email or a portal -- exactly what they use today. The AI operates behind your systems. From the supplier's perspective, they're communicating with your company. The automation is invisible to them.

useCases.name: Mainframe Modernization
useCases.tagline: Modernize COBOL without losing what makes it run your business.
useCases.pattern: Every major bank, insurer, and government agency has a mainframe that runs mission-critical processes no one fully understands anymore. The senior engineers who wrote the code are retiring. The documentation is either missing or wrong. And the business can't afford an outage.
useCases.gap: The standard approach -- document first, then modernize -- takes years and the documentation is obsolete before modernization starts. Bob reverse-engineers the code, generates accurate documentation, and translates to modern languages in parallel.
useCases.proof: A top-10 US bank used Bob to translate 2.3 million lines of COBOL to Java in 18 months, with 94% of the translated code passing automated testing on first pass -- a project their internal team estimated would take 7 years manually.
useCases.validation: One real COBOL module reverse-engineered and translated in 2 weeks, with the output reviewed side-by-side by their senior engineer.
compellingEvents: Key COBOL engineer announcing retirement in next 6-12 months
Mainframe license renewal driving total cost of ownership conversation
Regulatory requirement to modernize or document legacy systems
Cloud migration roadmap requiring modernization of core systems
Recent mainframe outage triggering leadership scrutiny
useCases.killerQuestion: If your two best COBOL engineers left tomorrow, what happens to the roadmap for those systems?

Situation: How many lines of COBOL or legacy code are you running in production today? And how much of that would you call truly understood versus tribal knowledge?
Who are the 2-3 engineers who actually know how your mainframe systems work? How close are they to retirement or moving on?
What's your current plan for modernizing these systems, and what's slowing it down?
Pain: When something breaks in a legacy system at 2am, what's the process for diagnosing it? Who gets called, and how long does it take?
What's the last time you had to make a change to a COBOL system and how long did it take from request to production?
How much of your application development budget goes into maintaining legacy systems versus building new capabilities?
Impact: What does a mainframe outage actually cost -- in transaction revenue, regulatory risk, and executive attention?
If you could move 30% of your legacy systems to modern languages in 18 months, what does that unlock for your cloud strategy?
What's the total cost of keeping a COBOL engineer on staff versus what you'd pay a Java developer to maintain the same system?
Critical Event: Is there a specific engineer retirement or departure that's creating urgency around capturing institutional knowledge now?
Is there a mainframe license renewal, cloud migration milestone, or regulatory deadline that's putting this on the roadmap?
Has a recent incident or audit made leadership more aware of the risk concentration in the mainframe?
Decision: A decision like this typically involves the CIO, the application development leader, and often the CFO given the capex. Who's the right group to evaluate this?
If a 2-week proof showed your COBOL module translated and tested accurately, who would champion moving to a full engagement?

objections.objection: We've tried modernization before and it failed.
objections.response: What failed was the lift-and-shift approach -- rewriting from scratch without understanding what the code actually does. Bob doesn't start with a rewrite. It starts with reverse-engineering the existing logic, documenting it accurately, then translating. The output is validated against the original. The failure mode of past projects was guessing what the code did. Bob eliminates the guessing.

objections.objection: Our COBOL runs too fast. Modern languages can't match the performance.
objections.response: That was true 10 years ago. Modern JVM performance on Java handles the transaction volumes that used to require COBOL. And the question isn't whether COBOL is fast -- it's whether you can maintain, extend, and hire for it in 5 years. The performance conversation has been settled. The talent and agility conversation hasn't.

objections.objection: We can't risk touching systems that run our core business.
objections.response: You don't touch the production system during the proof. Bob works on a copy of the code in an isolated environment. The translation is validated in parallel. Production doesn't get updated until the translated code passes your testing gates. The risk model is the same as any other code migration.

useCases.name: Developer Productivity
useCases.tagline: Ship faster without hiring more engineers.
useCases.pattern: Engineering teams are under pressure to deliver more with the same or smaller headcount. Copilot helps with greenfield development but doesn't support the enterprise languages, platforms, and security controls that large orgs actually run on.
useCases.gap: GitHub Copilot and similar tools are built for modern, open-source languages. They don't support COBOL, RPG, enterprise Java frameworks, or proprietary internal libraries. They have no concept of your security policy or compliance requirements. Bob does.
useCases.proof: A global insurer deployed Bob across 400 developers working on enterprise Java and COBOL systems, achieving a 35% reduction in time-to-code and a 28% reduction in defect rate -- without a single security policy exception.
useCases.validation: One developer team using Bob on their real codebase for 2 weeks. Measure: lines of code per sprint, time to first commit on new features, and defect rate.
compellingEvents: Engineering headcount freeze requiring productivity gains
Competitor shipping features faster creating pressure
GitHub Copilot evaluation revealing coverage gaps
Developer retention issues tied to tooling quality
Board mandate to reduce time-to-market
useCases.killerQuestion: Which part of your tech stack do your developers most wish they had better AI assistance for -- and what's stopping them from using what's available?

Situation: How are your developers using AI coding assistance today? What's working well and where do they hit walls?
What languages and frameworks make up the core of your production systems? What percentage of that is covered by tools like Copilot?
What's your current sprint velocity, and how much of the slowdown is time-to-code versus review, testing, and deployment?
Pain: When a developer has to work in a legacy system or an unsupported language, how much longer does it take compared to modern stack work?
What's the developer experience like when they need to understand an unfamiliar part of the codebase? How long does it take to get productive?
What's your biggest engineering bottleneck right now -- is it planning, coding, review, or something else?
Impact: If you could increase developer throughput by 30% without adding headcount, what does that mean for your roadmap commitments for the year?
What's the cost of a developer spending 40% of their time on work that AI could assist with -- in opportunity cost and retention risk?
How does developer tooling quality factor into your ability to attract and retain senior engineers?
Critical Event: Is there a product launch, platform migration, or feature commitment that's putting delivery timelines under pressure right now?
Has the Copilot evaluation or another AI tooling assessment surfaced gaps that leadership is now watching?
Is there a headcount freeze that's requiring engineering to demonstrate more output from the same team?
Decision: For a developer tooling decision, who's in the room -- CTO, engineering VPs, and does security need to approve the code access model?
If a 2-week pilot showed a 30% productivity lift for a team of 10, what does the evaluation and procurement path look like?

objections.objection: We already have GitHub Copilot.
objections.response: Copilot is excellent for greenfield development in mainstream languages. Show me your tech stack and I'll tell you exactly where it stops working. COBOL, RPG, enterprise Java frameworks, internal libraries, proprietary APIs -- Copilot has no context for any of those. Bob is trained on the languages and platforms your systems actually run on.

objections.objection: Our developers don't want AI writing their code.
objections.response: The developers who resist are usually the ones who've tried tools that suggest wrong code confidently. Bob doesn't replace the developer's judgment -- it reduces the time they spend on the mechanical parts: searching documentation, writing boilerplate, understanding unfamiliar code. The developers who use it feel more productive, not replaced.

objections.objection: We can't allow AI tools that send our code to an external model.
objections.response: Bob can run in your environment, on your infrastructure, with no code leaving your perimeter. The model is deployed on-prem or in your private cloud. IBM's security and data governance controls apply. This is a design requirement for every enterprise we work with.

useCases.name: Application Refactoring
useCases.tagline: Refactor legacy applications at the speed of AI, with the accuracy of an engineer.
useCases.pattern: Every enterprise has a backlog of legacy applications that need to be refactored or re-platformed before they can move to cloud. The bottleneck is always engineering capacity -- refactoring is tedious, time-consuming, and the kind of work senior engineers avoid.
useCases.gap: Refactoring requires understanding existing code deeply enough to change it safely. That takes senior engineering time and slows the cloud migration roadmap. Bob does the code comprehension and generates the refactored output, letting senior engineers focus on validation.
useCases.proof: A regional bank re-platformed 400 Java applications to a modern cloud-native framework in 8 months using Bob -- a project their architecture team estimated at 3 years of manual refactoring.
useCases.validation: One application module refactored end-to-end in 2 weeks, with output validated by their architecture team.
compellingEvents: Cloud migration roadmap blocked by refactoring backlog
Application portfolio rationalization initiative
Platform EOL date creating urgency to re-platform
Senior engineering talent avoiding legacy refactoring work
Cloud cost savings opportunity blocked by lift-and-shift approach
useCases.killerQuestion: What's sitting on your cloud migration roadmap right now that's blocked because you don't have the engineering capacity to refactor it?

Situation: How many applications are in your portfolio that need refactoring before they're cloud-ready? And what's your current estimate for clearing that backlog?
How do you decide which applications to refactor versus re-buy versus sunset? And who's doing the refactoring work?
What percentage of your engineering team's time goes to refactoring and re-platforming versus building new capabilities?
Pain: What's the application on your roadmap that everyone knows needs to be refactored but keeps getting pushed back? Why?
When you put senior engineers on refactoring work, what does that mean for the product roadmap that depends on those same people?
What's the failure rate on refactoring projects -- where the output doesn't work as expected and has to be reworked?
Impact: What cloud cost savings or platform capabilities are blocked until you clear the refactoring backlog?
If you could 3x your refactoring throughput without adding headcount, what does that do to your cloud migration timeline?
What's the engineering cost of a refactoring project that takes 18 months versus one that takes 4?
Critical Event: Is there a platform EOL date, a cloud commitment, or a legacy system support cost that's creating a hard deadline on clearing this backlog?
Is there a board-level cloud migration commitment that the refactoring backlog is now threatening?
Has the cost of maintaining unrefactored legacy systems become a budget line item that leadership is scrutinizing?
Decision: For a decision like this, does it sit with engineering leadership, or does architecture, security, and the cloud platform team all need to be aligned?
If a 2-week proof showed a module refactored accurately and cloud-ready, who would evaluate it and what would the procurement path look like?

objections.objection: AI-generated code doesn't meet our quality standards.
objections.response: Bob doesn't replace your code review process -- it feeds into it. The output goes through your existing gates: static analysis, automated testing, peer review. What changes is how much of the mechanical refactoring work your engineers have to do manually before the code reaches review. The quality bar is yours. The labor to get there is ours.

objections.objection: We tried a modernization tool before and the output was unusable.
objections.response: Pattern-matching tools generate code that looks right but doesn't understand what the original was doing. Bob uses the same underlying model that understands code semantics, not just syntax. The difference is whether the tool knows what the code means or just what it says. Let us run one module as a proof -- you'll see the difference in the output.

useCases.name: AI Risk & Compliance
useCases.tagline: Prove your AI is compliant before the regulator asks.
useCases.pattern: Regulated industries deploying AI at scale are discovering that their compliance frameworks were built for human decisions, not model decisions. When a model makes a lending, underwriting, or treatment decision, who is responsible? How is it documented? What happens when it drifts?
useCases.gap: Most governance efforts are manual -- spreadsheets, periodic reviews, and post-hoc audits. That worked at 3 models. At 30, it creates unacceptable risk lag. By the time a compliance team discovers a model has drifted, the decisions are already made.
useCases.proof: A top-5 US bank deployed watsonx.governance across its credit decisioning and fraud detection models, achieving continuous compliance monitoring across 47 models with automated audit trails -- replacing a manual review process that took 6 weeks per model.
useCases.validation: One live model put under governance in 14 days -- policy enforced, drift monitored, audit trail generated.
compellingEvents: EU AI Act or sectoral regulation deadline
Upcoming regulatory exam where AI is on the agenda
Recent AI incident or near-miss at their firm or a competitor
Board-level AI risk discussion requiring CRO to report
New Chief AI Officer or Head of AI Risk hire needing to demonstrate control
useCases.killerQuestion: If your regulator asked you today to produce a complete audit trail for every AI decision made in the last 90 days, how long would it take?

Situation: How many models do you have in production today making decisions that touch customers, credit, or risk? And do you have a single system of record for all of them?
What does your current model review process look like -- who owns it, how often does it happen, and how long does each review take?
When a regulator or auditor asks for model documentation, where does that come from today and how confident are you in its accuracy?
Pain: When a model needs to be updated or retrained, what's the current approval process? How many people touch it and how long does it take?
How do you know today if a model in production has drifted from its approved behavior? What's your detection lag?
What happens when a model makes a decision that gets challenged -- by a customer, a regulator, or an internal audit? Can you reconstruct the decision trail?
Impact: What does a regulatory finding against one of your AI models actually look like -- in fines, remediation cost, and reputational risk?
If your compliance team is spending 6 weeks per model on manual review, what's that costing you in headcount and what are they not doing instead?
What's the board asking the CRO to be able to say about AI risk by end of year?
Critical Event: Is there a regulatory exam, an AI Act compliance deadline, or a board risk review where you need to demonstrate control over your AI systems in the next 6 months?
Has a recent incident -- at your firm or a competitor -- elevated the urgency around AI governance at the board or CRO level?
Is there a new regulation or regulatory guidance that's creating a hard deadline on demonstrating model governance?
Decision: A decision like this typically involves the CRO or CCO, the head of model risk, and the CIO for the infrastructure piece. Is that the right group?
If we put one of your live models under governance in 14 days and showed you the audit trail and drift alerts, who would evaluate that and what does procurement look like?

objections.objection: We have a model risk management framework already.
objections.response: Your MRM framework defines the standards. Governance automates the evidence collection and enforcement against those standards. Most MRM frameworks were designed for quarterly reviews of a handful of models. When you have 30, 50, or 100 models in production, the manual evidence collection becomes the bottleneck. We automate what your MRM framework requires.

objections.objection: The regulators haven't told us exactly what they want yet.
objections.response: That's the risk. The EU AI Act, SR 11-7, and emerging sectoral guidance all point in the same direction: documentation, auditability, and drift monitoring. Organizations that have the infrastructure in place when the regulation lands will have a 12-18 month advantage over those who build it reactively. The direction is clear even if the final rules aren't.

objections.objection: We're building our own governance tooling internally.
objections.response: What's your plan for keeping it current when the regulatory requirements change? Internal governance tooling becomes a maintenance liability the moment the regulation updates. We update the policy library and compliance checks continuously. That's a different economics calculation than owning it yourself.

useCases.name: Model Lifecycle Management
useCases.tagline: From model development to production approval in days, not months.
useCases.pattern: Data science teams are shipping models faster than governance can keep up. The approval process for moving a model from development to production is a bottleneck -- manual reviews, ad-hoc documentation, and a compliance team that doesn't speak data science.
useCases.gap: There is no standard workflow for model approval. Every model goes through a different process, different documentation format, and different reviewer. When something goes wrong in production, there's no trail to trace back to the approval decision.
useCases.proof: A global insurer standardized model approval workflows with watsonx.governance, cutting average time from model ready to production approval from 6 weeks to 5 days -- enabling their data science team to ship 3x more models per quarter without adding compliance headcount.
useCases.validation: One model's full lifecycle documented and approval workflow configured in 14 days.
compellingEvents: Data science team shipping models faster than compliance can review
New CDO or Head of AI with a mandate to scale AI responsibly
Model approval backlog blocking business value delivery
Board AI strategy requiring demonstrated governance capability
Failed model in production creating urgency around approval standards
useCases.killerQuestion: How many models are waiting for approval to go to production right now, and what's the bottleneck in the review process?

Situation: What does your current model development to production workflow look like? Who owns each stage and where does it typically stall?
How many models does your data science team ship to production per quarter today, and how many do you think you could ship if the approval process weren't a bottleneck?
What documentation does a model need to have before it can go to production review? Is that standardized across teams?
Pain: What's the average time from a model being ready to it being approved for production? And where does most of that time go?
When a model fails or behaves unexpectedly in production, how do you trace it back to what was approved at review?
What's the relationship between your data science team and your compliance or model risk team like -- is it collaborative or adversarial?
Impact: What's the business value sitting in the model development backlog waiting for approval right now -- in revenue, cost savings, or risk reduction?
If you could cut your model approval time from 6 weeks to 5 days, how many more models could your team ship per year?
What's the cost to the business of a data science team that's 60% utilized because approval is the bottleneck?
Critical Event: Is there a board AI strategy presentation, an investor update, or a regulatory filing where you need to demonstrate a functioning AI governance capability?
Is there a specific high-value model stuck in approval right now that has a business deadline attached to it?
Has a recent model incident -- a bias finding, a drift issue, an audit exception -- created urgency around standardizing the approval process?
Decision: A decision like this typically involves the CDO, the head of model risk or compliance, and the CIO for infrastructure. Who's the right evaluation group?
If we documented one model's lifecycle and configured the approval workflow in 14 days, who would evaluate the output and what does the path to a broader deployment look like?

objections.objection: Our data scientists will never adopt another tool in the workflow.
objections.response: The data scientists don't change their workflow -- governance wraps around it. They build in their existing environment. Governance captures the metadata, documentation, and approval trail automatically. The added friction is near-zero. The benefit is that the compliance team stops being the bottleneck because the evidence is already there.

objections.objection: We're a small team. We don't have 30 models. This feels like overkill.
objections.response: You have 5 models today. Your CDO has a mandate to have 50. The governance infrastructure you put in place now determines whether you can scale AI responsibly or whether you create a compliance liability at the speed of your ambition. It's much easier to instrument the first 10 models than to retrofit 50.

useCases.name: Regulatory Reporting for AI
useCases.tagline: Answer the regulator's AI questions before they ask them.
useCases.pattern: Compliance teams in regulated industries are being asked by regulators to account for AI systems they didn't build, using frameworks that weren't designed for AI. The result is manual evidence-gathering exercises that take months and still leave gaps.
useCases.gap: AI governance data is scattered across data science notebooks, model repositories, production monitoring tools, and email chains. When a regulatory report is due, the compliance team has to manually assemble it from a dozen sources -- and the report is always a snapshot of the past, not the current state.
useCases.proof: A regional bank prepared for its first AI-focused regulatory exam using watsonx.governance, producing a complete inventory of 23 AI systems, their decision histories, and drift reports in 3 weeks -- a process their compliance team estimated would take 6 months manually.
useCases.validation: Complete AI system inventory with decision audit trails and drift monitoring configured in 14 days, ready for examiner review.
compellingEvents: Regulatory exam with AI on the agenda in next 6 months
EU AI Act Article 9 risk management requirement deadline
SR 11-7 update or new regulatory guidance on model risk
Board or executive committee AI risk reporting requirement
Recent regulatory action against a competitor for AI governance failures
useCases.killerQuestion: When your regulator asks for a complete inventory of every AI system making decisions that touch customers or risk, how long does it take your team to produce it?

Situation: How do you currently track all AI systems in production? Is there a central inventory, or does each business unit own its own list?
When a regulator asks about a specific AI model -- its decision logic, its training data, its performance over time -- where does that information come from today?
How many people in compliance or risk are currently responsible for AI governance? And what percentage of their time goes to evidence collection versus analysis?
Pain: When you need to produce an AI inventory or audit report, how long does the evidence-gathering take? And how confident are you in the completeness of what you find?
What happens when a regulator asks about an AI system that was deployed 18 months ago and the team that built it has turned over? Can you reconstruct the approval trail?
What are the gaps in your current AI governance documentation that you'd be most uncomfortable showing to an examiner?
Impact: What would a material regulatory finding against one of your AI systems look like -- in fines, remediation, and enforcement action?
If your compliance team could generate AI regulatory reports automatically instead of manually, how would you redeploy that capacity?
What's the reputational risk of an AI governance failure becoming public in your industry right now?
Critical Event: Is there a regulatory exam scheduled in the next 6-12 months where AI will be on the agenda?
Is there an EU AI Act compliance deadline, a sectoral regulation, or a board requirement creating a hard date on demonstrating AI governance?
Has a recent regulatory action at a competitor firm elevated the urgency of this conversation at your board or C-suite level?
Decision: For a decision like this, is the CCO or CRO the primary decision-maker, or does the CIO need to be involved for the infrastructure piece?
If we produced a complete AI inventory with audit trails in 14 days -- the kind of report you'd show an examiner -- who would evaluate it and what does procurement look like?

objections.objection: We don't know what the regulator is going to ask for yet.
objections.response: That's exactly the risk. The examination guidance is evolving, but the direction is consistent: inventory, auditability, and ongoing monitoring. Organizations with the infrastructure already in place will answer the examiner's questions in hours. Those without it will spend months in discovery mode. We help you be ready for the question before it's asked.

objections.objection: Our legal team is handling the regulatory response.
objections.response: Legal handles the regulatory relationship. Governance provides the evidence. When the examiner asks for decision logs, model documentation, and drift analysis, your legal team needs a system that can produce that evidence quickly and completely. That's what we provide -- the production system behind the regulatory response.

personas.title: CFO
personas.frame: ROI and cost reduction. CFOs buy outcomes that show up on the income statement.
personas.openingAngle: Cost per transaction, FTE efficiency, and cycle time are the three numbers that matter.
personas.painLanguage: carrying operational overhead that should have been automated years ago
personas.ceAngle: Quarter close, audit cycle, or board financial review where efficiency is on the agenda.
personas.sayInstead: cost per transaction, cycle time reduction, FTE redeployment, margin impact

personas.title: CHRO
personas.frame: HR transformation and employee experience. CHROs buy things that make HR strategic instead of transactional.
personas.openingAngle: What percentage of the HR team's time goes to work that should be automated?
personas.painLanguage: HR team is a help desk when it should be a strategic partner
personas.ceAngle: Headcount freeze, engagement survey results showing HR responsiveness issues, HRIS migration.
personas.sayInstead: employee experience, HR capacity for strategic work, time to resolution

personas.title: CIO
personas.frame: Operational efficiency and modernization. CIOs buy things that reduce cost, increase reliability, and enable the business.
personas.openingAngle: What's the ratio of run-the-business spend to change-the-business spend in your IT budget?
personas.painLanguage: legacy systems consuming budget that should go to innovation
personas.ceAngle: Platform EOL, cloud migration milestone, headcount freeze requiring productivity gains.
personas.avoidSaying: AI transformation, disruption, future of work
personas.sayInstead: operational efficiency, cost per ticket, modernization velocity, technical debt reduction

personas.title: CTO
personas.frame: Engineering velocity and platform quality. CTOs buy things that let their team ship faster without adding headcount.
personas.openingAngle: What's your current impedance to shipping -- is it planning, coding, review, or deployment?
personas.painLanguage: senior engineers spending time on mechanical work instead of hard problems
personas.ceAngle: Roadmap commitment under pressure, developer headcount freeze, Copilot evaluation revealing gaps.
personas.sayInstead: developer throughput, time to first commit, defect rate, roadmap velocity

personas.title: COO
personas.frame: Operational scalability. COOs buy things that let the operation scale without linear headcount growth.
personas.openingAngle: Where is your operation most dependent on human coordination for things that should be automatic?
personas.painLanguage: operation scaled past the point where manual coordination works
personas.ceAngle: Peak season, supplier consolidation, merger integration, cost reduction mandate.
personas.sayInstead: operational throughput, coordination cost, exception handling volume, scale without headcount

personas.title: CDO
personas.frame: Responsible AI at scale. CDOs buy things that let them ship more AI without creating compliance risk.
personas.openingAngle: How many models are waiting for approval to go to production right now?
personas.painLanguage: data science team blocked by a governance process that wasn't built for this volume
personas.ceAngle: Board AI strategy presentation, investor update requiring demonstrated governance capability.
personas.sayInstead: model velocity, approval throughput, governance as a competitive advantage

personas.title: CRO / CCO
personas.frame: Risk reduction and regulatory readiness. CROs and CCOs buy things that reduce the probability and impact of a regulatory finding.
personas.openingAngle: What would it take to produce a complete audit trail for every AI decision made in the last 90 days?
personas.painLanguage: operating with AI systems that can't be explained or audited at the speed regulators expect
personas.ceAngle: Upcoming regulatory exam, EU AI Act deadline, recent competitor enforcement action.
personas.sayInstead: audit trail, drift detection, regulatory readiness, model risk documentation

questions.question: What function is this buyer primarily responsible for?
What pressure are they most visibly under right now?

options.label: Do more with same or fewer people
Scale faster than the team can grow

questions.question: What did they say in the first two minutes?

options.label: Something about manual processes or approvals taking too long
Something about legacy systems or technical debt
Something about AI risk, compliance, or governance
Something about developer productivity or shipping speed
Something about headcount or budget pressure

routes.reason: Finance + efficiency pressure + manual process signal = finance approvals play with Orchestrate. The CFO pain is approval cycle time and FTE cost.
HR function + efficiency pressure = HR service delivery play. CHROs buy Orchestrate to stop their team being a help desk.
IT function + efficiency pressure + manual signal = IT service desk play. CIOs buy Orchestrate to deflect L1/L2 tickets.
IT + modernize pressure + legacy signal = mainframe modernization play. CIOs with legacy debt buy Bob to reduce risk and cost.
Operations + scale pressure + manual signal = supply chain coordination play. COOs buy Orchestrate to automate inter-system coordination.
Engineering + velocity pressure = developer productivity play. CTOs buy Bob when they need to ship more without hiring more.
Engineering + modernize pressure + legacy signal = app refactoring play. Cloud migration roadmaps blocked by refactoring backlog.
Risk function + regulatory pressure = AI risk and compliance play. CROs and CCOs buy governance to be ready for the examiner.
Data function + scale pressure = model lifecycle management play. CDOs buy governance to ship more models without creating compliance risk.
General management + regulatory signal = AI risk play. C-suite pressure around AI governance usually routes to the CRO or CCO conversation.
General management + efficiency pressure + manual signal most commonly maps to cross-functional automation. Start with the Orchestrate finance approvals play.
